coredata.dc:title: Conductive thin-film nanocomposite nanofiltration membrane comprising N-doped graphene quantum dots with relieved concentration polarization for sulfate separation from high-salinity solution
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Feng
Miao, Ju
Xu, Lina
Zhou, Quanzhu
Deng, Tianlong
coredata.dc:description: Nanofiltration (NF) has been an environmentally-friendly technique for separating sulfate from concentrated seawater in the Chlor-alkali industry. However, the commercial thin-film composite (TFC) NF membranes suffered more deteriorated concentration polarization, membrane fouling and the perm-selectivity trade-off effect in practical application. Here, a thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) NF membrane with conductivity and low cross-linking degree was constructed by the synergistic manipulation of N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), dopamine and piperazine on the nanostructure of active polyamide layer. With the high-salinity solution containing 15 g·L−1 Na2SO4 and 127 g·L−1 NaCl as feed solution, the well-designed TFN-NF membrane exhibited Na2SO4 rejection of 99.70% and achieved synchronous improvement in water permeability (173.02 L·m−2 h−1 MPa−1, 3.62 times of the pristine TFC membrane), water/Na2SO4 selectivity and NaCl/Na2SO4 selectivity under an electric field. After the cyclic humic acid fouling-regeneration process three times, it still possessed a water permeance recovery ratio (FRR) of 98.55%. Moreover, the improved wettability and higher ratio of quaternary amine groups (N+H3-C) derived from the N-GQDs covalently linked to the polyamide layer endowed it with excellent antibacterial activity. The multifunctional TFN-NF membrane demonstrated its potential application in high-salinity solution separation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Nanofiltration membrane
High-salinity solution
Concentration polarization
Membrane fouling

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Molecular weight cut-off and pore size distribution
Micro-nano structural characterization of N-GQDs doped TFN membrane
Separation performance of N-GQDs-doped TFN membrane

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of the top surface of the TFC and TFN membranes. (a) The common TFC membrane denoted as pristine PA membrane, (b) DA/PA membrane and (c) N-GQDs+DA/PA membrane.

Structural characterization of the N-GQDs-doped TFN membranes. (a) XRD patterns, (b) dynamic water contact angle (WCA), (c) linear sweep voltammetry curves and (d) TGA and derivative thermo-gravimetric (DTG) curves of the N-GQDs+DA/PA, DA/PA and pristine PA membranes.
Chemical properties of the N-GQDs-doped TFN membranes. (a) FT-IR spectra and (b) XPS curves of N-GQDs+DA/PA, DA/PA and pristine PA membranes.
Deconvolution of the XPS spectra. (a-c) C1s, (d-f) N1s and (g-i) O1s for (a, d, g) pristine PA, (b, e, h) DA/PA and (c, f, i) N-GQDs+DA/PA membranes.
Pore structure of the TFN membranes. (a) MWCO and (b) pore size distribution.

NF separation performance of the N-GQDs-doped TFN membranes. (a) Comparison of pure water permeance of the N-GQDs+DA/PA, DA/PA and pristine PA membranes; (b) water permeance and Na2SO4 rejection (R%) of the N-GQDs+DA/PA with various concentrations of Na2SO4 as the feed solution; (c) different salts rejection and selectivity of salt/Na2SO4 and (d) the effect of an electric field on the separation performance of N-GQDs+DA/PA for the high-salinity solution containing 15 g·L−1 Na2SO4 and 127 g·L−1 NaCl.

Antifouling performance of the N-GQDs-doped TFN membranes. (a) Comparison of the water permeance during the cyclic fouling-regeneration process; (b) Na2SO4 permeance recovery and rejection rate; (c) Schematic diagram of the interaction between the membrane surface and HA.

Antimicrobial property of the N-GQDs-doped TFN membranes against (a) E. coli and (b) S. aureus. For comparison, the pristine PA, N-GQDs/PA and DA/PA membranes were also tested with the same sample diameter of 6.00 mm. The width of the inhibition circle was calculated along the outer edge of the sample, assisted by an optical microscope.

Illustration of the fabrication of N-GQDs-doped TFN membrane. Interfacial interaction occurred between N-GQDs+DA+PIP and TMC. Pink: PIP; Brown: PDA; Red: N-GQDs; Blue: TMC. (For interpretation of the references to color in this scheme legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Chemical composition of the TFN membranes obtained from the deconvolution of XPS spectra.
Comparison of N-GQDs-doped TFN membrane with the current NF membranes in literature for high-salinity solution separation.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Data was not given in the literature.

head.ce:title: Conductive thin-film nanocomposite nanofiltration membrane comprising N-doped graphene quantum dots with relieved concentration polarization for sulfate separation from high-salinity solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Marine Resource Chemistry and Food Technology, Ministry of Education, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Marine Resources and Chemistry, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, 300457 Tianjin, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Marine Resource Chemistry and Food Technology
Tianjin Key Laboratory of Marine Resources and Chemistry
Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Marine Resource Chemistry and Food Technology, Ministry of Education, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Marine Resources and Chemistry, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, 300457, Tianjin, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: BTR New Material Group Co., Ltd., 518106 Shenzhen, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: BTR New Material Group Co., Ltd.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: BTR New Material Group Co., Ltd., 518106, Shenzhen, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanofiltration (NF) has been an environmentally-friendly technique for separating sulfate from concentrated seawater in the Chlor-alkali industry. However, the commercial thin-film composite (TFC) NF membranes suffered more deteriorated concentration polarization, membrane fouling and the perm-selectivity trade-off effect in practical application. Here, a thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) NF membrane with conductivity and low cross-linking degree was constructed by the synergistic manipulation of N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), dopamine and piperazine on the nanostructure of active polyamide layer. With the high-salinity solution containing 15 g·L−1 Na2SO4 and 127 g·L−1 NaCl as feed solution, the well-designed TFN-NF membrane exhibited Na2SO4 rejection of 99.70% and achieved synchronous improvement in water permeability (173.02 L·m−2 h−1 MPa−1, 3.62 times of the pristine TFC membrane), water/Na2SO4 selectivity and NaCl/Na2SO4 selectivity under an electric field. After the cyclic humic acid fouling-regeneration process three times, it still possessed a water permeance recovery ratio (FRR) of 98.55%. Moreover, the improved wettability and higher ratio of quaternary amine groups (N+H3-C) derived from the N-GQDs covalently linked to the polyamide layer endowed it with excellent antibacterial activity. The multifunctional TFN-NF membrane demonstrated its potential application in high-salinity solution separation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: High-salinity solution, especially concentrated seawater after desalination until saturated, has ever been the preferred raw material of the national Chlor-alkali industry [1,2]. During the Chlor-alkali process, separating sulfate anion (SO4 2−) from the high-salinity solutions is the key procedure for obtaining the refined products and reducing harmful imprints [3,4]. Nanofiltration (NF), as an advanced membrane technology for mono/multi-valent ions separation based on collaborative mechanisms of size sieving and Donnan exclusion, has become a promising and environmentally-friendly technique [5]. As the core of NF technology, thin-film composite (TFC) NF membranes composed of a porous substrate and a cross-linked polyamide (PA) separation layer have dominated 80% of the membrane market because of their relatively high separation performance and low fabrication cost compared with cellulose acetate membranes and are mainly applied in low-salinity solutions (500–2000 ppm) [6,7]. However, the commercial NF membranes utilized for high-salinity solutions suffer from severe perm-selectivity trade-off effect, deteriorated concentration polarization and membrane fouling arising from inorganic salts, organic compounds and biological adhesion and irreversible blockage of membrane pores [8]. Yuan [9] et al. refined saturated brine by commercial DL2540 NF membrane, which maintained SO4 2− rejection of 99.9%, but its water permeance rapidly attenuated from 6.18 to 0.74 L·m−2 h−1 MPa−1 with the increasing of brine salinity due to the reduced driven force caused by the accumulation of inorganic salts in the substrates (internal concentration polarization) or the active PA layer (external concentration polarization).

To overcome these problems, tailored microstructures of advanced NF membranes [10] and optimization of the membrane separation process [11] are urgently desired. On the one hand, thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane has been tremendously investigated since first proposed by Hoek (2007) [12], where novel nanomaterials coupled with inherent cavity and enriched reactive groups, such as graphene oxide [13], graphene quantum dots [14], carbon nanotubes [15], metal-organic frameworks [16] and covalent organic frameworks [17] were incorporated into the active PA layers for regulating their crossing-linked networks and chemical composition. In the previous works, authors have constructed multifunctional TFN membranes by doping TpPa-2 nanosheets [7], COF-COOH [18] and polydopamine [19] to manipulate their chemical structure, pore size distribution and pore connectivity, endowing them with improved water permeability, water/salt selectivity, and antibacterial activity. Another strategy for alleviating pore blocking caused by membrane fouling and concentration polarization is electrically interfering with the separation process. Typically, Tan [20] et al. intelligently fabricated an electrically pore-size-tunable polypyrrole membrane with relieved membrane fouling from humic acid by an applied potential on the basis of a 21.91% increased specific flux. Recently, novel charged TFN-NF membranes featuring high hydrophilicity and antifouling performance were first proposed based on spontaneous polarization of ferroelectric BaTiO3 nanoparticles, exhibiting 2.4 times water permeability and favorable arsenic rejection of 97% compared with common TFC membranes [21]. Inspired by these state-of-the-art works, the precision construction of conductive TFN membranes might shed light on their application potential for high-salinity solution separation.

Among the advanced nanomaterials, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) are carbon-based nanomaterials with a quantum size of below 20 nm, high conductivity, hydrophilicity and chemical affinity [22]. Based on the structural merits, N-GQDs have served as nanofillers to enhance the stability, water perm-selectivity, and rejection of organic molecules of TFC membranes for desalination and pervaporation [23,24]. Thus, N-GQDs might be the ideal functional material to prepare a conductive TFN-NF membrane for effective sulfate separation from a high-salinity solution.

In this work, we adopted a two-in-one strategy to construct conductive and loose TFN-NF membranes comprising N-GQDs for efficient SO4 2− separation from high-salinity solution assisted by increasing repulsion between inorganic salts (or other pollutants) and membrane surface under an external electric field. For the well-designed TFN membranes, the relationships among membrane microstructure (such as pore size distribution, conductivity and hydrophilicity), selective separation performance and fouling resistance were systematically explored. This work would lay a technical foundation for the utilization of the novel TFN-NF membrane for high-salinity solution in the Chlor-alkali process.

Piperazine (PIP, 99%, Sigma-Aldrich), dopamine hydrochloride (DA, 98%, Sigma-Aldrich), trimesoyl chloride (TMC, 98%, Sigma-Aldrich), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, ≥99.9%, Aladdin), lithium chloride (LiCl, ≥98%, Aladdin), n-hexane (≥ 97%, Sinopharm), humic acid (HA, ≥99%, Sinopharm), polysulfone (PSF, 80 kDa, Udel P-1700, USA) and polyethylene glycols (PEG, Rhawn). Inorganic salts of Na2SO4, MgSO4, MgCl2 and NaCl were purchased from Sinopharm. Deionized (DI) water with a conductivity of 18.25 μs·cm−1 was obtained by the Aquapro water purification system. N-GQDs were supplied by BTR New Material Group Co., Ltd., Shenzhen, China.

A certain amount of N-GQDs was dispersed in tris solution using a probe of ultrasonic homogenizer (FS-150N, Shanghai Shengxi Supersonic Instrument Co., Ltd.) under 600 W until obtaining a homogeneous dispersion. The concentration of N-GQDs aqueous solution was measured to be approximately 15.8 mg·mL−1 via the weight method. PIP (1 wt%) and DA (0.1 wt%) were added to the N-GQDs dispersion assisted by an ultrasonic homogenizer (FS-150 N, Shanghai Shengxi Supersonic Instrument Co., Ltd.) for 5 min and then spread on PSF substrate (with a smaller pore size of 7.76 nm than N-GQDs, Fig. 1a, Figs. S1–3) by pressure assistance for the formation of N-GQDs+DA+PIP cushion. Subsequently, TMC/n-hexane solution was added and the interfacial polymerization reaction occurred for 30 s, followed by rinsing with excess n-hexane and heat treatment at 80 °C for 60 s (Scheme 1 ). The as-synthesized TFN membrane was recorded as N-GQDs+DA/PA. For comparison, the TFN membrane doped with DA (recorded as DA/PA) and the TFC membrane (recorded as pristine PA) were also prepared.

For the N-GQDs-doped TFN membrane and pristine TFC membrane, surface morphologies were observed by Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM, S-4800, Hitachi, Japan). The crystal structure was examined using X-ray diffraction (XRD, Smartlab, Rigaku, Japan). Thermal stability was determined by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). Membrane conductivity (3 cm × 3 cm) was measured by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV, electrochemical workstation CHI600E, Shanghai Chenhua Instruments Co., Ltd., China) with the voltage from 0.0 to 0.8 V at 10 mV·s−1. Surface hydrophilicity was evaluated by a contact angle measuring instrument (DSA 100, Kruss, Germany). Chemical structure and elemental composition were measured by Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR, Nicolet 6700, USA) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific, K-Alpha, USA). The PEG and salt concentrations in permeate were tested by a total organic carbon analyzer (TOC, Shimadzu, Japan) and inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES, Teledyne Leeman Labs, USA), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular weight cut-off and pore size distribution
ce:section.ce:para:
Electroneutral PEG (50 mg·L−1) with various molecular weights of 200–1000 Da was used for evaluating the pore size of the membrane. The membrane sample with an area of 9.08 cm2 was fixed in a cross-flow filtration setup. After fully compacting, it was tested at 0.6 MPa and 20 ± 2 °C. The molecular weight cut-off of the TFN-NF membranes was determined according to PEG rejection (R s) - molecular weight (MW) curve at R s of 90% [25]. R s was obtained based on Eq. (1), where C fs and C ps were the PEG concentrations in feed and permeated solution, respectively. The relationship between PEG diameter (d s) and its MW conformed to Eq. (2) [26]. Based on the probability density function as Eq. (3), the pore size distribution was obtained, where μ p was the mean pore size calculated as R s = 50% and σp was the geometric standard deviation by the ratio of d s at R 0 = 84.13% and R 0 = 50% [27].

2

Water permeance (J w), salt rejection (R%), salt permeance (J s) and water/salt selectivity (α) [28] were tested at 0.6 MPa using Na2SO4 solutions (with concentrations of 3, 6, 15, 25 and 30 g·L−1) and mixed solution (containing Na2SO4 of 15 g·L−1 and NaCl of 127 g·L−1) as the feed solutions.
(4)

Δ P − Δ π
where Q was the quantity of the permeate over a certain time (Δt). A m was the membrane area, ΔP was the external pressure and Δπ was the osmosis pressure.

The rejection for Na2SO4, MgCl2, MgSO4, LiCl and NaCl with a concentration of 2000 ppm was also tested. The salt/Na2SO4 selectivity (S) [29] of the membrane was defined as the rejection ratio of other salt and Na2SO4 and calculated by Eq. (8).
(8)

1 −
R salt
1 −
R
Na 2
SO 4
where R Na2SO4 (%) and R salt (%) were the rejection of Na2SO4 and other salts, respectively.

To explore the anti-fouling property of the N-GQDs-doped TFN membrane, HA was used as the simulated organic pollutant. Before pollution testing, the membrane samples were pre-compacted using DI water until obtaining steady pure water permeance and then taking 15 g·L−1 Na2SO4 as feed solution. The water permeance (J w1) and Na2SO4 rejection were calculated based on Eq. (4) and Eq. (5). Subsequently, 5 mg·L−1 HA solution was injected into the feed solution. After 1 h, the membrane was thoroughly cleaned using DI water until the absorbance of the permeate was 0 and then converted 15 g·L−1 Na2SO4 as feed solution. The water permeance of the membrane after fouling was denoted as J w2. Finally, the water permeance recovery ratio (FRR) was calculated based on Eq. (9). The membrane fouling-recovery processes were repeated at least three times.
(9)
FRR
%
=
J
w 2
J
w 1
× 100 %

Antimicrobial efficiency of the TFN membrane was evaluated using the “zone of inhibition” method [30]. Prior to the test, all materials were fully sterilized using high-pressure steam cooker. Circle membranes with a diameter of 6.0 mm were laminated on the solid Luria-Bertani (LB) plates, where were coated the testing microorganisms of Escherichia coli (E.coli.) or Staphylococcus aureus (SAU). After incubating in a shaking incubator (HNY-200B, Tianjin Honour instrument CO., Ltd) at 37 °C for 24 h, an optical microscope (CH20BIMF200, Olympus Optical Co., Ltd) was used to calculate the width of bacteriostatic circles around the membrane samples and the bacterial distribution along their edges.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Micro-nano structural characterization of N-GQDs doped TFN membrane
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, N-GQDs acted as the functional nanomaterials to manipulate the micro-nano structure of the active PA layer by doping them into the PIP+DA aqueous phase and simultaneously conducting interfacial polymerization with TMC/hexane on the homemade porous PSF substrates (Scheme 1). Different from the common nodular structure of the TFC membranes (Pristine PA, Fig. 1a) [31], the synergistic interaction of DA and PIP would regulate the elastic structure and hydrophilicity of active PA layer and present more rough PA networks (DA/PA, Fig. 1b), which was consistent with the previous work [19]. When further doped with N-GQDs, much larger and coarser nodule structures formed on the surface of the PA layers (N-GQDs+DA/PA, Fig. 1c), which would contribute to increasing the effective surface area and wettability, thus improving water permeability of the TFN membranes during separation process [32]. Besides the wide peak at around 2θ = 20° from the XRD pattern (Fig. 2a), it presented an extra diffraction peak at the higher degree of ca. 2θ = 27° consistent to the N-GQDs (Fig. S5a), indicating the covalent existence of N-GQDs in the amorphous PA layer.

The incorporation of N-GQDs into the PA layer modified its wettability, conductivity and thermal stability. The improvement in the wettability of the N-GQDs-doped TFN membrane was confirmed by the dynamic water contact angle (WCA) [33]. As seen in Fig. 2b and Fig. S4, the N-GQDs+DA/PA membrane exhibited a much lower dynamic WCA of 11.9–8.8° within 180 s than those of 28.0–21.3° for the pristine PA and 20.1–12.9° for DA/PA membranes, respectively. Due to the basal planes of conjectured sp2 hybridization carbon atom [34,35], the N-GQDs incorporation endowed the PA layer with an efficient electric channel and possessed a significantly higher current of 0.05–0.90 A with the increasing voltage from 0.0 to 0.8 V (Fig. 2c). The improved conductivity (or declined membrane resistance, Table S2) made it the possibility to achieve repulsion of inorganic salt and pollutants in separation process under an electric field. Based on the TGA and DTG curves (Fig. 2d, Table S3), similar weightlessness trends were presented for the N-GQDs+DA/PA, DA/PA and pristine PA layers because of their same bulk nature of polyamide. However, during the degradation stage, the N-GQDs+DA/PA layer showed a higher inflection point of 471 °C than DA/PA layer (456 °C) and pristine PA layer (445 °C). The improvement of thermal stability after N-GQDs incorporation might be attributed to its role as a radical scavenger for effectively delaying the degradation temperature [36], suggesting the improved interfacial interactions between N-GQDs and polymer matrix [7].

The formation of the loose structure of PA layer after N-GQDs incorporation was further confirmed by calculating molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) and pore size distribution. As seen in Fig. 5a, incorporating N-GQDs endowed the PA layer with a larger MWCO of 646 Da than 385 Da for DA/PA membrane. Meanwhile, the N-GQDs+DA/PA membrane showed a broader pore size distribution and a larger mean pore size of 0.62 nm than DA/PA membrane (0.50 nm), which could theoretically reject the transport of hydrated sulfate ion with a diameter of 0.76 nm (Table S4) when neglecting the hydrodynamic interaction between sulfate ion and pores (Fig. 5b). It indicated that N-GQDs+DA/PA layer possessed a relatively loose structure on the basis of its integrity and selectivity for salt separation [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation performance of N-GQDs-doped TFN membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The manipulation of N-GQDs on the nanostructure of DA/PA layer directly affected its water permeability, salt rejection and water/salt selectivity. As presented in Fig. 6a, the incorporation of N-GQDs triggered a significant improvement in water permeability with a pure water permeance of 164.63 L·m−2 h−1 MPa−1. Interestingly, when applied an external potential during the separation process, its pure water permeance further increased to 173.02 L·m−2 h−1 MPa−1, which was 3.62 times for the pristine PA membrane and 2.65 times for the DA/PA membrane. The positive effect on water permeability might be attributed to the tailored nanostructure of PA layer by the synergistic interaction of DA and N-GQDs. DA used as one of the aqueous monomers and hydrophilic modification material at the same tine could decrease the cross-linking degree and increase the hydrophilicity of active PA layer (Fig. 2b, Fig. 4). The incorporation of N-GQDs into the DA/PA layer endowed the membrane with conductivity, further improvement in its hydrophilicity and charge property (Fig. 2b,c). These tailored properties caused by N-GQDs and DA would promote water transport through the membrane. When further applying an electric field, the charged and hydrophilic groups such as amino, hydroxyl groups, etc., in the N-GQDs-doped NF membrane would probably tend to enrich on the pore surfaces and electrically tune the instantaneous increase of its pore size and hydrophilicity, further enhancing the water permeance. Moreover, it was inferred that the enrichment of the charged and hydrophilic groups on the membrane surface under an electric field might also strengthen the electrostatic repulsion between salts and the negatively charged membrane surface (Fig. 7c, Fig. S9) and reduce the driven force caused by the accumulation of inorganic salts on membrane surface so as to relieve the concentration polarization. This hypothesis was confirmed by its separation performance for Na2SO4 with various concentrations from 3 to 30 g·L−1. As seen in Fig. 6b, all the testing membranes retained a comparable Na2SO4 rejection of above 92%, though showing a slight decline with the increase of Na2SO4 concentration. However, the water permeance of the pristine PA membrane rapidly attenuated from 38.38 to 1.83 L·m−2 h−1 MPa−1 caused by severe concentration polarization [40]. In contrast, due to the tailored nanostructure and conductivity of PA layer by N-GQDs (Fig. 2, Fig. 4), the N-GQDs+DA/PA membrane still presented a satisfactory water permeance of 57.48 L·m−2 h−1 MPa−1 though with the concentrated Na2SO4 of 30 g·L−1 as the feed solution. Its improved H2O/Na2SO4 selectivity (α) of 15.72, compared to 9.94 for pristine PA membrane and 10.40 for DA/PA membrane (Fig. S8) would be attributed to the proper reduction on the cross-linking degree of PA layer caused by the synergistic interaction among N-GQDs, DA and PIP [41] and the relieved concentration polarization, helping the simultaneous improvement of water permeability and H2O/Na2SO4 selectivity for the TFN membranes in high-salinity solution separation process.

The salt/Na2SO4 selectivity of the N-GQDs-doped TFN membrane was also investigated. As seen in Fig. 6c, it exhibited different salt rejections with the order of Na2SO4 (99.70%) > MgSO4 (87.23%) > MaCl2 (46.66%) > LiCl (38.22%) > NaCl (22.20%). The higher sulfate rejection was mainly attributed to the larger hydration radius by steric hindrance and the stronger repulsive effect for its more negatively charged membrane surface (Fig. S9) [41]. To further evaluate its effectiveness in high-salinity solution separation, when using a mixed salt solution containing 15 g·L−1 Na2SO4 and 127 g·L−1 NaCl as the feed solution, it performed an excellent water permeance of 77.60 L·m−2 h−1 MPa−1 with a comparable Na2SO4 rejection of 99.70% under an electric field better than its separation performance tested in natural condition without electric field (Fig. 6d). However, it showed a compromised separation performance in the mixed solution because of its higher osmotic pressure and diffusion resistance compared to that in a single salt solution. Considering the main coexistence of Na2SO4 and NaCl in the feed solution for the Chlor-alkali industry, the high NaCl/Na2SO4 selectivity (S) of 259.33 for the N-GQDs-doped TFN membrane was well expected, compared to those (S = 8.06–57.50) for the commercial TFC-NF membranes (Table 2 ). This provided the implemented availability by increasing the repulsion effect between inorganic salts and conductive membrane surface for improving the perm-selectivity of TFN-NF membranes for high-salinity solution in the Chlor-alkali process.

Presently, tremendous strategies such as regulating the substrate structure [3], incorporating functional materials [40], post-modification [44,45] and so on have been adopted for alleviating the concentration polarization of TFC-NF membranes. Table 2 summarizes the separation performance of current TFC-NF membranes for Na2SO4 in a high-salinity solution. Though using a higher concentration mixed salt solution as the feed solution than those in literature, the N-GQDs-doped TFN membrane still performed a superior Na2SO4 rejection of 99.70% compared to the reported values ranging from 85.00% to 99.78%. Meanwhile, it showed a dominant pure water permeance of 173.02 L·m−2 h−1 MPa−1 (compared to 55–130 L·m−2 h−1 MPa−1 in literature), synchronous coupled with a comparable H2O/Na2SO4 selectivity of 332.33 and NaCl/Na2SO4 selectivity of 259.33. By comparison, the well-designed N-GQDs-doped TFN membrane possessed structural and performance advantages in high-salinity separation applications.

Presently, membrane fouling arising from inorganic salts, organic compounds and biological adhesion exerted serious membrane permeance attenuation due to the irreversible blockage of membrane pores and destruction of membrane structure [51]. This work used HA as the representative organic pollutant for antifouling testing. After being contaminated by HA solution, the N-GQDs+DA/PA membrane maintained an uncompromising Na2SO4 rejection of 97.21% and a water permeance recovery ratio (FRR) of 98.55%. Applying an external electric field further promoted the increase of water permeance recovery ratio to 99.09% and presented a comparable Na2SO4 rejection of 96.45% though experiencing a cyclic fouling-regeneration process three times (Fig. 7a,b). On closer observation of the membrane surface after HA fouling, fewer pollutants deposited on the membrane surface when applying an electric field, probably owing to the stronger electrostatic repulsion between negatively charged membrane surface and HA foulants (Fig. 7c, Fig. S9) [52].

Besides the resistance to inorganic and organic pollution, the construction of novel NF membranes with antimicrobial ability is also a great demand for the membrane separation industry [53]. Here, E. coli. and S. aureus were used as the testing bacteria for evaluating the antimicrobial efficiency of the N-GQDs-doped TFN membrane by inhibition zone and colony-forming count method. As seen in Fig. 8 , compared with E. coli., a larger inhibition circle width of 2.69 mm against S. aureus was presented on the N-GQDs-doped TFN membrane surface than that of 1.05 mm when against E. coli. It might be related to the lower isoelectric point of (pH = 2–3) for S. aureus than E. coli (pH = 4–5), causing stronger electrostatic repulsion between S. aureus and the negative membrane (Fig. S10,11) [54]. Whether for E. coli or S. aureus, the N-GQDs+DA/PA membrane performed a higher antimicrobial efficiency than DA/PA and pristine PA membranes. Taking E. coli as an example, with the incorporation of DA and N-GQDs, their antimicrobial efficiency gradually increased with the width of the inhibition circle of 0.82 mm, 1.00 mm and 1.05 mm for pristine PA, DA/PA and N-GQDs+DA/PA membranes, respectively. It might be attributed to the significant improvement in membrane hydrophilicity that inhibited the accumulation of hydrophobic bacteria [55,56]. Meanwhile, the higher ratio of the quaternary amine groups (N+H3-C) with the function of decomposing bacterial membranes for both N-GQDs+DA/PA (22.42%) and DA/PA layers (13.91%) in Table 1 would also contribute to the stronger antimicrobial capacity of membranes [44], further demonstrating the synergistic interaction among N-GQDs, DA and PIP on the tailored nanostructure and antifouling functionality of PA layer.

In summary, conductive and relatively loose TFN-NF membranes comprising N-GQDs were conducted for efficient sulfate separation from high-salinity solution assisted by increasing repulsion between inorganic salts (or other pollutants) and membrane surface under an external electric field. With the high-salinity solution containing 15 g·L−1 Na2SO4 and 127 g·L−1 NaCl as feed solution, the well-designed TFN-NF membrane exhibited Na2SO4 rejection of 99.70% and achieved improvement synchronously on water permeability (up to 173.02 L·m−2 h−1 MPa−1, 3.62 times of the pristine TFC membrane), H2O/Na2SO4 selectivity of 332.33 and NaCl/Na2SO4 selectivity of 259.33 under an electric field. After the cyclic HA fouling-regeneration process three times, it still possessed a water permeance recovery ratio (FRR) of 98.55% with an uncompromising Na2SO4 rejection of 97.21%. Moreover, the improved wettability and higher ratio of quaternary amine groups (N+H3-C) derived from the N-GQDs covalently linked to the polyamide layer endowed it with excellent antibacterial activity. The multifunctional TFN-NF membranes shed light on their potential application for sulfate separation from concentrated seawater in the Chlor-alkali industry.

Feng Guo: Experiment, Visualization and Writing-Original Draft. Ju Miao: Experiment and Methodology. Lina Xu: Ideas, Writing-Reviewing and Editing. Quanzhu Zhou: Resources. Tianlong Deng: Funding acquisition and Reviewing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22208245 and 22073068), the China National Nuclear Corporation (KHNY-FW-GKXJ-21-0021) and the Yangtze Scholars and Innovative Research Team of the Chinese University (grant number IRT_17R81).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Supplementary Information (SI) was available: preparation and characterization of PSF substrate, digital photos of the time-dependent water contact angle and the antimicrobial properties for N-GQDs-doped TFN membrane, selectivity of H2O/Na2SO4, zeta potential, data analysis of resistance value and TGA results, etc.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary Information (SI) was available: preparation and characterization of PSF substrate, digital photos of the time-dependent water contact angle and the antimicrobial properties for N-GQDs-doped TFN membrane, selectivity of H2O/Na2SO4, zeta potential, data analysis of resistance value and TGA results, etc. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.desal.2023.116526.
